title: Stronger than "good"
why_grouped_en: Beginners lean on "good" and "nice" for everything. These five say the same idea with more colour — but each has a slightly different strength and feel. Learn them together so you can pick the right one.
why_grouped_mm: "good" တစ်လုံးတည်း အမြဲသုံးမနေပါနဲ့။ ဒီစကားလုံးတွေက ပိုပြီး အားကောင်းတယ်။

words.short_mm: အရမ်းကောင်းတယ်
words.explanation_en: The everyday step up from "good". Safe in almost any situation — spoken or written, casual or work.
words.nuance_en: Neutral and extremely common. When in doubt, "great" works.

examples.en: We had a great time at the beach.

words.short_mm: အံ့သြစရာ ကောင်းတယ်
words.explanation_en: Stronger than "great". Use it when something is so good it almost surprises you.
words.nuance_en: Emotional and enthusiastic — great for reactions, a bit much for formal writing.

examples.en: The view from the top was amazing.
She did an amazing job on the project.

words.short_mm: အရမ်းကို ကောင်းတယ်
words.explanation_en: A warm, upbeat "very good". Common in speech to show you're pleased.
words.nuance_en: Friendly and positive. Interchangeable with "great" but a notch more excited.

examples.en: The food at that restaurant is fantastic.

words.short_mm: အရည်အသွေး မြင့်တယ်
words.explanation_en: "Very good" with a focus on quality or standard. Slightly more formal — good for work and feedback.
words.nuance_en: Sounds professional. You'd write "excellent work" on a report, not "amazing work".

words.short_en: very good and makes you feel happy
words.short_mm: နှစ်သက်ဖွယ် ကောင်းတယ်
words.explanation_en: "Very good" with warmth and feeling. Used about experiences, people, and moments more than objects.
words.nuance_en: Heart-felt. "a wonderful person" sounds kind; "a wonderful laptop" sounds odd.

examples.en: We had a wonderful evening together.

exercises.prompt: On a job reference: "She has ___ attention to detail."
Reacting to a surprise: "You climbed the whole mountain? That's ___!"
About a kind person: "My grandmother was a truly ___ woman."
Safe, everyday choice: "That's a ___ idea, let's do it."
